stem: A continuous random variable \( X \) has probability density \( f(x) = [[c]]\,x^{[[m]]} \) for \( 0 \le x \le [[b]] \) (and \( f(x) = 0 \) elsewhere). What is \( P([[lo]] \le X \le [[hi]]) \)?
solution: Integrate the density over the interval: \( P([[lo]] \le X \le [[hi]]) = \int_{[[lo]]}^{[[hi]]} [[c]]\,x^{[[m]]}\,dx = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This integrates \( x^{[[m]]} \) but forgets to multiply by the density constant [[c]].
This forgets that integrating raises the exponent, using \( x^{[[m]]} \) values instead of \( x^{[[m]]+1} \).
This treats \( X \) as uniform on \( [0, [[b]]] \), ignoring the actual density.
This omits dividing by \( [[m]]+1 \) after integrating.
This is \( P(X \le [[hi]]) \), the CDF at the upper limit, ignoring the lower limit.
This is \( P(X \le [[lo]]) \), the CDF at the lower limit, not the interval probability.

stem: A continuous random variable \( X \) is distributed uniformly on the interval \( [[[a]], [[b]]] \). What is \( P([[lo]] \le X \le [[hi]]) \)?
solution: For a uniform density, probability is proportional to length: \( P([[lo]] \le X \le [[hi]]) = \frac{[[hi]] - [[lo]]}{[[b]] - [[a]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This divides the segment length by 2 instead of by the total interval width.
This is just the segment length, forgetting to divide by the total width at all.
This inverts the correct ratio.
This is \( P(X \le [[hi]]) \), the CDF at the upper limit, ignoring the lower cutoff.
This is \( P(X \le [[lo]]) \), the CDF at the lower limit, not the requested interval.
This uses the wrong denominator \( [[b]] - [[lo]] \) instead of the full interval width.

stem: A continuous random variable \( X \) has the triangular (tent) density \( f(x) = \dfrac{[[b]] - |x - [[b]]|}{[[b]]^{2}} \) for \( 0 \le x \le 2\cdot[[b]] \) (and \( f(x) = 0 \) elsewhere), which rises linearly to a peak at \( x = [[b]] \) and falls linearly back to \( 0 \) at \( x = 2\cdot[[b]] \). What is \( P([[lo]] \le X \le [[hi]]) \)?
solution: Integrate the tent density over the interval (splitting at the peak \( x=[[b]] \) if the interval crosses it): \( P([[lo]] \le X \le [[hi]]) = \int_{[[lo]]}^{[[hi]]} \frac{[[b]] - |x-[[b]]|}{[[b]]^2}\,dx = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This treats the density as uniform on \( [0, 2\cdot[[b]]] \), ignoring the triangular shape entirely.
This uses the wrong (unsquared) normalizing denominator.
This mishandles the absolute value by treating the density as purely linear (\(x/b^2\)) rather than tent-shaped.
This evaluates only the density's HEIGHT at the upper bound, not the accumulated area.
This evaluates only the density's HEIGHT at the lower bound, not the accumulated area.
This squares the segment length instead of integrating the density.

stem: A continuous random variable \( X \) has the exponential density \( f(x) = \lambda e^{-\lambda x} \) for \( x \ge 0 \), where \( \lambda = \frac{[[lamn]]}{[[lamd]]} \). What is \( P([[lo]] \le X \le [[hi]]) \)?
solution: The CDF of an exponential variable is \( F(x) = 1 - e^{-\lambda x} \), so \( P([[lo]] \le X \le [[hi]]) = F([[hi]]) - F([[lo]]) = e^{-\lambda [[lo]]} - e^{-\lambda [[hi]]} = [[answer]] \), matching direct integration of the density.
distractor_rationales: This has the correct two terms but is missing the sign flip from integrating \(-\lambda e^{-\lambda x}\) correctly (an artifact of the antiderivative's sign).
This has the two exponential terms in the wrong order (sign error).
This is \( P(X \le [[hi]]) \), the CDF at the upper bound alone, ignoring the lower cutoff.
This is \( P(X > [[lo]]) \), the survival function at the lower bound, not the interval probability.
This is the survival function at the upper bound alone.
This treats the density as uniform, multiplying the rate by the segment length.

stem: A continuous random variable \( X \) taking values in \( [0, [[b]]] \) has cumulative distribution function \( F(x) = \left(\dfrac{x}{[[b]]}\right)^{[[n]]} \). Using \( P(a \le X \le c) = F(c) - F(a) \), what is \( P([[lo]] \le X \le [[hi]])\)?
distractor_rationales: This is \( F([[hi]]) \) alone, forgetting to subtract \( F([[lo]]) \).
This is \( F([[lo]]) \) alone, not the difference.
This raises the RATIO of the difference to the power \( n \), instead of subtracting the two individually-raised CDF values.
This adds the two CDF values instead of subtracting them.
This differentiates the CDF form incorrectly, using a linear scaling by \( n \) instead of the actual power-law CDF values.
This uses the density's linear (uniform-style) ratio instead of the given power-law CDF.

stem: A continuous random variable \( X \) has cumulative distribution function \( F(x) = \left(\dfrac{x}{[[b]]}\right)^{3} \) for \( 0 \le x \le [[b]] \) (equivalently, density \( f(x) = \dfrac{3x^2}{[[b]]^3} \)). If \( F(t_0) = [[p]] \) for some \( t_0 \) in \( [0, [[b]]] \), what is \( t_0 \)?
solution: Since \( F(t_0) = \left(\dfrac{t_0}{[[b]]}\right)^3 = [[p]] \), taking the cube root of both sides gives \( t_0 = [[b]]\cdot [[p]]^{1/3} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This treats the density as if it were uniform, scaling \( t_0 \) linearly by the target probability instead of taking a cube root.
This squares the target probability instead of taking its cube root.
This finds the complementary point (as if the target probability were a survival probability).
This uses the complement of the target probability with the same linear (uniform-style) mistake.
This takes a square root instead of a cube root.
This mixes a linear scale-up by \( b^2 \) with the raw probability, the wrong dimensional combination.
